title: The New Trade Order: Restoring a Broken Global Economy
content:
At the annual meeting of the World Economic Forum in Davos, dozens of senior officials discussed the urgent need for a new trade order to restore balance to a broken global economy. A decade ago, a populist wave of anger at the status quo began to fundamentally challenge the international system. Today, for the first time in a long time, the United States is facing genuine, intense competition from state-sponsored economies. Former and current leaders agree that the old models of globalization are no longer sufficient to protect national interests. Consequently, policymakers are exploring new frameworks to navigate this complex era of geoeconomic rivalry.

The Chinese economic system relies heavily on massive subsidies to fuel intense competition, where many firms fail but a few emerge as dominant global champions. In this model, short-term inefficiency and massive capital misallocation are tolerated in service of long-term strategic gains. Unlike in democratic systems, there are no voters asking their elected representatives whether their tax dollars could be put to better use. Historically, the United States dismissed the Chinese system as too rigid for true frontier innovation. However, the undeniable success of this model in certain sectors has forced American leaders to reconsider their own technological strategies.

The American system—democratic and capitalist, with world-class universities and strong intellectual property protections—has produced a dazzling lineup of transformative breakthroughs. While the United States cannot and should not replicate China’s authoritarian approach, Americans must find their own way to compete on this broader playing field. It is no longer enough to simply innovate; the nation must also produce advanced technology and control vital supply chains. These critical inputs are necessary to power the domestic economy and secure the broader defense industrial base. Updating the American system requires a targeted strategy to establish and protect enduring structural advantages.

An effective American technology strategy must focus on revitalizing its techno-industrial base to generate massive production capacity across resilient supply chains. The U.S. military must also prioritize rapid innovation and adaptation, which are critical for deterring aggression and maintaining stability across regions like the Taiwan Strait. Furthermore, Washington must build a democratic digital order that establishes American technology as the prevailing global model. This model must be protected by high safety standards, financed transparently, and deeply respectful of human rights and data privacy. Together, these efforts will create a robust foundation for future economic and military security.

Finally, this comprehensive approach must strive to establish a floor of stability in the complex U.S.-Chinese relationship. Meaningful cooperation between the two powers is occasionally necessary to prevent a destructive race to the bottom that ultimately hurts everyone. These strategic high grounds are mutually reinforcing, as a revitalized techno-industrial base directly supports both military power and economic prosperity. By carefully balancing fierce competition with pragmatic diplomacy, the United States can secure its position in the new global order. The resulting framework will protect democratic values while ensuring long-term national resilience.
